title: Coconut and lime soy candle
subtitle: A tropical-scented soy container candle with coconut fragrance and lime essential oil
excerpt: A classic soy container candle with a two-note fragrance: coconut fragrance oil for warmth and depth, and lime essential oil added at cooldown for a sharp citrus top note that would otherwise evaporate at full pour temperature.
sourceNotes: Standard soy container candle method from CandleScience and Brambleberry. Two-stage fragrance addition technique for preserving high-volatility essential oils.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Pour multiple jars in one session; soy wax works well in batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best left to cure for 48 hours before burning for the best scent throw.
recipe.temperatureNote: Pour at 55 to 60 degrees Celsius. Add lime essential oil at cooldown (55 degrees Celsius) to preserve the top note.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months. Citrus scents fade faster than woody or floral fragrances. Store in a lidded jar or tin when not in use.

glossaryTerms.definition: A soft-grade soy wax formulated to adhere to the sides of a jar or tin as it sets, producing a clean, even finish. It has a lower melting point than pillar-grade wax and is not suitable for free-standing candles.
The percentage of fragrance oil by weight added to a wax. Soy container wax typically holds 6 to 8% fragrance load.

The 24 to 48 hour resting period after a soy candle sets, during which the fragrance fully binds to the wax crystals. A properly cured candle has a significantly stronger scent throw than one burned immediately after pouring.
The pool of liquid wax that forms around a burning wick. A full melt pool extends to the edges of the jar; achieving this on the first burn prevents tunnelling.

items.prepNote: To fill a 250 ml jar, allowing for shrinkage
6% of wax weight; added at 65 degrees Celsius
2% of wax weight; added at cooldown (55 degrees Celsius)

content.text: Secure the wick to the bottom of the jar using the metal tab (a small blob of hot glue or a wick sticker works). Hold the wick upright with a wick centring tool or two pencils across the rim. Melt the soy wax in a double boiler to 70 to 75 degrees Celsius. Remove from heat. At 65 degrees Celsius add the coconut fragrance oil and stir for 2 minutes. At 55 degrees Celsius add the lime essential oil and stir gently.
Pour into the jar at 55 to 60 degrees Celsius. Leave to set undisturbed for at least 8 hours. Trim the wick to 5 mm before the first burn. Allow 48 hours cure before burning.

Candles keep for 12 months. Store with the lid on when not in use to preserve the citrus scent and prevent dust settling in the wax. Trim the wick to 5 mm before every burn. Never burn for more than 4 hours at a time. Never leave unattended.
